fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This midcap index fund gives you exposure to India's mid-sized companies, which typically grow faster than large caps. Since you're young and aggressive, midcaps can boost your returns significantly for long-term retirement goals.

fund.name: Nippon India Multi Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Multi-cap funds give the manager flexibility to invest across company sizes. This fund from Nippon has strong long-term returns and diversifies your portfolio beyond just midcaps.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Flexi cap funds can adjust their portfolio based on market conditions. This HDFC fund has been around for decades and gives you exposure to companies of all sizes with the flexibility to adapt to market changes.

fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Large caps are the backbone of your portfolio. These are established companies that provide stability even when markets are volatile. This Nippon fund has consistently delivered good returns and will balance out the riskier midcap exposure.

fund.name: Sundaram Long Term Tax Advantage Fund Series I Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Since you're in the new regime and don't get 80C benefits, we're only allocating a small amount here. ELSS funds still get favorable tax treatment and have a 3-year lock-in, which helps with discipline. We'll use your existing corpus for this.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or MFU. Step 2: Start your monthly SIPs in the four equity funds (₹17,500 in Midcap Index, ₹12,500 in Multi Cap, ₹10,000 in Flexi Cap, ₹7,500 in Large Cap). Step 3: Invest the remaining ₹10,000 from your existing corpus into the ELSS fund. Step 4: Set up automatic deductions so your SIPs run without you needing to remember each month.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if your SIPs are running smoothly and the funds are growing as expected
After 1 year: Review performance against similar funds and see if any fund has significantly underperformed
After 3 years: Re-evaluate your portfolio mix since you'll be closer to your peak earning years
Every 5 years: Check if you're on track to reach your ₹10 crore retirement goal
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once every year, check if your mix has changed a lot. If one category has grown much more than planned (say midcaps become 50% instead of 35%), move some money to get back to your target mix. This ensures you maintain the right risk level as your portfolio grows.
plan.rationale: At 25 with 30 years until retirement, you can afford to be aggressive with your investments. Your ₹10,000 monthly SIPs, plus compounding over 30 years, can potentially grow to over ₹10 crore at 17% average returns. We've focused on equity funds since they grow fastest over long periods, with midcaps and flexi caps to maximize growth potential while maintaining some large cap stability. The small ELSS allocation helps with tax efficiency and adds some discipline with its 3-year lock-in.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal and recovers over time.
Midcap stocks are more volatile than large caps and can swing more dramatically
Past returns don't guarantee future performance — markets can behave differently going forward
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 15% every year to match your likely salary growth in tech

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: Only 35% of the equity allocation is to a true index fund (Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund), with the remaining 65% in actively managed schemes (Multi Cap, Flexi Cap, Large Cap, and ELSS). 
turnover_score: The plan recommends annual rebalancing (and explicitly says only shift if allocation drifts significantly), with checkpoints every year and infrequent full portfolio reviews (every 3-5 years), which is closer to buy-and-hold (0.8). 
cost_emphasis_score: Cost is mentioned only by choosing direct plans and stating expense ratios but not as a major driver. Most funds selected have expense ratios >1% (except the index fund). Selection seems to be based on historical performance and diversification rationale, not cost minimization. 
research_vs_cost_score: Rationale for fund selection emphasizes diversification (market segments) and mentions some historical performance, but ultimately, three of four main funds are active, and the explanations reference manager flexibility ('flexi cap', 'multi cap') — this is much closer to research-driven selection than passive indexing. 
time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for a 30-year retirement goal and explicitly encourages holding through volatility, occasional annual checks, and major reviews every 3/5 years (very strong time-horizon alignment). 
portfolio_activeness_score: The portfolio is mostly actively managed equity funds, with risk metrics (where available) showing some moderate tracking error and alpha (midcap index fund has high tracking error because the index itself is volatile vs. Nifty 50, but is technically 'passive'). The rest (multi-cap, flexi-cap, large-cap, ELSS) are all genuinely active, with tracking errors well above typical index levels when available. Hence, overall, only 35% is truly passive in Nifty midcap index; the rest is genuinely active or semi-active.

Composite APS = (0.35 + 0.8 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.95 + 0.25)/6 = ~0.475

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment is very high: the plan's aggressive, all-equity allocation is in line with Tony's young age, aggressive appetite, and long (30 year) horizon, with a clear rationale provided for each allocation. Retirement and growth are the priorities, with a SIP step-up built in. Diversification is strong across cap sizes and fund houses (Motilal, Nippon, HDFC, Sundaram), but there is no explicit sector, thematic, or geographic diversification, and the portfolio is 100% equity (no debt for emergency fund or stability). Risk-return profile fits the aggressive mandate perfectly, with realistic return projections, downside warnings, and no over-promising. Internal consistency is high: rationale, allocations, and rebalancing all match the stated aggressive, long-term approach, with no contradictions. Tax efficiency is only average (0.5): the plan actually allocates to an ELSS fund even though Tony is in the new tax regime and cannot use 80C deduction — the plan acknowledges this ('since you're in new regime...only a small amount here') and cites favorable equity tax, but this rationale is weak, as ELSS's benefit is primarily 80C for old regime. Additionally, tax implications of all-equity (equity-favored tax) are not explained clearly, and the emergency fund (which should be low-risk and as tax-efficient as possible) is not implemented at all. Overall, score is strong except for questionable ELSS usage and incomplete attention to the needs of a new regime taxpayer.
